Event: David Fairchild was born in Lansing, Michigan. His parents were George Fairchild, who was president of Kansas State University and the Agricultural College of Michigan, and Charlotte Pearl Halsted Fairchild, who founded the Domestic Science Club of Kansas State University.
Source: The Food Explorer: Book Ch.1; The world was my Garden; Kansas State University library

Event: After studying at the College of Agriculture at Kansas State University, Fairchild joined the USDA. At that time, however, his work was not related to plant exploration or introductions, but rather to plant pathology.

After obtaining a Smithsonian fellowship in Naples, Fairchild departs for Europe on the SS Fulda. On board, he has a brief encounter with Barbour Lathrop, who will later become his patron and expedition partner.
As Fairchild studies at the Naples Zoological Station, Lathrop offers him $1,000 USD per year to accompany him on his travels.

On December 17 in Corsica, Fairchild attempts his first plant introduction without authorization or funding (the Secretary of Agriculture had refused both); he is briefly arrested before sneaking four citron bud-sticks into his coat pocket and escaping the island
Fairchild travelled to Java to study termites at the botanical gardens in Buitenzorg (now Bogor).
Fairchild and Lathrop visit Sumatra together
Fairchild visits Australia during Pacific tour with Lathrop, notes eucalyptus as a potential plant introduction.

Fairchild visits Fiji with Lathrop. There, he tries kava, the juice of the root of the Piper methysticum plant; which is used for cultural and recreational purposes in Oceania. In traditional medicine, kava is used to treat anxiety and stress.
Fairchild returns to Washington and co-founds the Office of Seed and Plant Introduction with Walter Swingle.
Fairchild and Lathrop begin their second world tour by collecting chayote and mangoes in Jamaica.

In Santiago, Fairchild packs nearly 1,000 frost-hardy black-skinned avocados and ships more than 10 boxes to Washington; the Chilean variety, prized for its consistent size and frost tolerance down to 23°F, would become a founding ancestor of the California avocado industry and ultimately the Hass avocado
Fairchild and Lathrop cross the Andes; collect bella sombra tree in Argentina
Fairchild collects Itamaraca mango
At a London market Fairchild picks up Windsor broad beans (fava beans) in peak late-summer season; a woman offers him a puree of the bean, one of his earliest encounters with hummus

Dining alone in Venice, Fairchild tries broccoli for the first time and pays the café's chef for a handful of seeds along with sweet peppers; notably, Walter Swingle had already shipped a primitive form from France months earlier; Fairchild was not the official first introducer of broccoli to the US
Fairchild collects sultanina (Thompson) seedless grapes at Padua

Crop: Egyptian cotton (Jannovitch); sesame; garbanzo beans; berseem clover
Event: Staying at Cairo's Shepheard's Hotel, Fairchild assembles his largest shipment to date, totalling 43 crop types including Jannovitch long-fiber cotton (that would help launch a southwestern American cotton industry worth $100 million by WWII), sesame, garbanzo beans, and berseem clover

During a 40-day cruise of the Malay Islands, Fairchild collects a pomelo, one of Earth's few original citrus ancestors (Citrus maxima), from the island of Sekar by charming the island's radja in a tense and potentially dangerous encounter

Fairchild collects the carabao mango on the outskirts of Manila, a fruit so saccharine and buttery its genes found their way into nearly every American mango variety bred over the following century; later marketed as the champagne mango
Fairchild collects rice varieties, water chestnuts, and guava in Canton

After befriending the secretive Bohemian hop-growing community, Fairchild covertly obtains 100 cuttings of the prized Semš hop variety; they arrive in America on December 18 and eventually help the US brewing industry surpass European rivals, aided further by WWI devastating Europe's hop fields
Fairchild collects grape varieties, carob, and hazelnuts in Italy

Along the Austrian coast, Fairchild encounters capuzzo, a two-thousand-year-old leafy cabbage the rest of the world would call kale; he ships it for trial in the American South, prophetically noting how easily it grows; kale would go on to become a nutritional phenomenon a century later

Fairchild pays $15 per tree for six fully grown pistachio trees, the first budded pistachio trees ever to exist in America, plus lentils; he also tracks down seedless lemons on the island of Poros after a tip from a German botanist

In Bombay, Fairchild builds the most extensive mango collection assembled to that point, approximately 80 varieties centered on the elite Alphonso, for trial in Florida and other warm American territories

Fairchild attempts to collect mangosteen in Saigon, the fruit he considered the finest in the world; he also secures Saigon mango seed; despite lifelong efforts he never succeeded in establishing mangosteen in American soil

Fairchild travels to Baghdad and collects date palm offshoots and dryland staples from Mesopotamia, the birthplace of agriculture; the date palms would help establish the date industry in the American Southwest
Fairchild collects nectarines in Quetta (then British India)

Crop: Wasabi; loquat; zoysia grass; persimmon; flowering cherry trees; udo
Event: Fairchild visits Japan and collects a wide range of edible and landscape plants; his first encounter with flowering cherry trees plants the seed for what will become one of his most lasting contributions, the iconic cherry blossoms lining the Tidal Basin in Washington D.C., gifted by Japan in 1912

Crop: Kaffir orange; carissa plum; miniature pineapple
Event: Fairchild and Lathrop embark on their last major botanical expedition together, circumnavigating Africa; they collect Kaffir orange, carissa plum, and a miniature pineapple before Fairchild returns to Washington in August 1903

Fairchild marries Marian Bell, daughter of inventor Alexander Graham Bell; the couple met through the National Geographic Society in 1903; their Maryland estate would later serve as a retreat for President Woodrow Wilson and his war cabinet during WWI
Frank Meyer hired by Fairchild after interview at USDA office
Frank Meyer departs for first China expedition (3 years); first assignment: find grindstone persimmon in Beijing

Meyer discovers the Meyer lemon near Beijing, a citrus hybrid combining the sweetness of a mandarin with the tartness of a lemon; the fruit he ships back will bear his name and become widely beloved in American cooking

Crop: Oats; millet; watermelon; cotton; wild pears; persimmons; Manchurian spinach; asparagus; soybeans; bamboo (30 types); red blackberries; wild apricots; zelkova trees; Chinese holly; white-barked pines; lilacs (18 forms); viburnum; spirea; rhododendron; daphne; lilies; lawn sedge
Event: Meyer first expedition: walks 20+ miles/day across China, Manchuria, Korea, and eastern Siberia over 3 years. Sends first soybeans and asparagus to officially enter the US. Returns in 1908 with 20 tons of plant material via Standard Oil steamer

Crop: Brazilian cherries; cashew wine; guava jelly; jaboticaba
Event: Wilson Popenoe departs for Brazil in search of a superior navel orange; he returns with Brazilian cherries, cashew wine, guava jelly, and jaboticaba grapes, though only a fraction pass quarantine inspection and the navel orange proves to have no equal

Crop: Walnuts; hazelnuts; chestnuts (blight resistant); jujubes; pears; apples; peaches; haw
Event: Meyer third expedition: discovers Chinese chestnut trees resistant to blight devastating American chestnuts.

Wilson Popenoe spends three years riding thousands of miles on horseback through Guatemala, studying thousands of avocado trees and living on beans and chayotes; he returns with 23 of the finest varieties packed in tin tubes with oiled paper to clear quarantine
Fairchilds buy property in Coconut Grove on Biscayne Bay; Lathrop names it the Kampong

Meyer's fourth and final expedition; suffering severe mental breakdown amid WWI-era chaos and rebellion in China, he makes one last collection, ginkgo biloba seeds and Ichang lemons, before dying under mysterious circumstances in the Yangtze River valley
First Frank Meyer Medal for plant introduction awarded to Barbour Lathrop

David Fairchild dies on August 6 at the Kampong in Coconut Grove under a Javanese ficus tree, at age 85; over his career he introduced more than 200,000 plant species and varieties to the United States
